data.main_text:
Koe koostuu neljästä osasta: kuullun ymmärtäminen, luetun ymmärtäminen, sanasto ja rakenteet sekä kirjallinen tuottaminen.



Voit tehdä tehtävät haluamassasi järjestyksessä. Noudata tehtäväkohtaisia vastaamisohjeita. Voit muuttaa antamiasi vastauksia koska tahansa kokeen aikana. Varaa aikaa kuullun ymmärtämisen tehtävien tekemiseen yhteensä noin 1 tunti.



Älä jätä mitään merkintöjä sellaisen tehtävän vastaukselle varattuun tilaan, jota et halua jättää arvosteltavaksi.



Onnea kokeeseen!

questions.main_text: The researchers extracted DNA from the skins of two preserved Christmas Island rats and sequenced it many times over to get as much of the genome as possible and used the genome of the Norway rat as a reference. Comparing the two genomes revealed that [SELECT id=78]. Many of those genes will be the ones that make each species unique, says Victoria Herridge from the Natural History Museum in London. She notes that the more time that has passed since an extinct species and its living relative have diverged, the more genes are likely to remain unknown. The work “really highlights the difficulties, maybe even the ridiculousness, of de-extinction efforts,” she says.

content.text: The researchers extracted DNA from the skins of two preserved Christmas Island rats and sequenced it many times over to get as much of the genome as possible and used the genome of the Norway rat as a reference. Comparing the two genomes revealed that [SELECT id=78]. Many of those genes will be the ones that make each species unique, says Victoria Herridge from the Natural History Museum in London. She notes that the more time that has passed since an extinct species and its living relative have diverged, the more genes are likely to remain unknown. The work “really highlights the difficulties, maybe even the ridiculousness, of de-extinction efforts,” she says.

choices.text: almost 5% of the Christmas Island rat’s genome was still missing
relying on the Norwegian rat as the only point of reference was problematic
the Norwegian and Christmas Island rats had almost identical genomes

questions.main_text:
#### Is it ok to cheat? 99 p.

Your school’s debate club has a debate on the topic of cheating and whether it is ever acceptable. People have discussed cheating from the point of view of job applications and con artists. Talk about cheating in a specific context and give your reasons for why cheating is ok or not ok. Write your speech.



*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

content.text:
#### Is it ok to cheat? 99 p.

Your school’s debate club has a debate on the topic of cheating and whether it is ever acceptable. People have discussed cheating from the point of view of job applications and con artists. Talk about cheating in a specific context and give your reasons for why cheating is ok or not ok. Write your speech.



*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

questions.main_text: “I had a tough time grappling with the sorts of executive functioning that our world operates by, like [SELECT id=117] to set up meetings, follow through with things, focus and be detail oriented,” he says. His manager had pointed [SELECT id=118] these failings for months, which is why his termination was hardly shocking.

content.text: “I had a tough time grappling with the sorts of executive functioning that our world operates by, like [SELECT id=117] to set up meetings, follow through with things, focus and be detail oriented,” he says. His manager had pointed [SELECT id=118] these failings for months, which is why his termination was hardly shocking.

questions.main_text: In the new study, Tom Gilbert, an evolutionary geneticist at the University of Copenhagen, and his colleagues, thought it best to start small. “If we want to try something so crazy, [SELECT id=76],” Gilbert reasoned. So, the researchers focused on the Christmas Island rat (*Rattus macleari*), which disappeared by 1908, because it is closely related to the Norway rat, a well-studied lab animal with a complete genome sequence that scientists already know how to modify.

content.text: In the new study, Tom Gilbert, an evolutionary geneticist at the University of Copenhagen, and his colleagues, thought it best to start small. “If we want to try something so crazy, [SELECT id=76],” Gilbert reasoned. So, the researchers focused on the Christmas Island rat (*Rattus macleari*), which disappeared by 1908, because it is closely related to the Norway rat, a well-studied lab animal with a complete genome sequence that scientists already know how to modify.

choices.text: let’s take a very unusual animal
why not start with a simple model
it’s best to use an extinct species

questions.main_text: The federal Greens remain [SELECT id=101] about the issue of giving First Nations people a more powerful say in national affairs. The reason for this is that the Victorian Greens have so far refused to outline their position on the Voice, despite making noises and being [SELECT id=102] about other federal issues that do not fall within the jurisdiction of state governments.

content.text: The federal Greens remain [SELECT id=101] about the issue of giving First Nations people a more powerful say in national affairs. The reason for this is that the Victorian Greens have so far refused to outline their position on the Voice, despite making noises and being [SELECT id=102] about other federal issues that do not fall within the jurisdiction of state governments.

questions.main_text: No species has yet been revived, but de-extinction appeals to many geneticists and futurists. “I know a lot of biologists who think, [SELECT id=73]” says Karen Wendling, an ethicist at the University of Guelph. Part of the fascination is simply the promise of seeing a vanished species come to life. But putting a key animal back into its original habitat could [SELECT id=74]. The mammoth once kept arctic shrubs and trees under control and fertilized grasses with their manure.

content.text: No species has yet been revived, but de-extinction appeals to many geneticists and futurists. “I know a lot of biologists who think, [SELECT id=73]” says Karen Wendling, an ethicist at the University of Guelph. Part of the fascination is simply the promise of seeing a vanished species come to life. But putting a key animal back into its original habitat could [SELECT id=74]. The mammoth once kept arctic shrubs and trees under control and fertilized grasses with their manure.

questions.main_text: preparation and practice can lessen it. Studies show extroverts enjoy more advantages in the workplace, which puts the burden on introverts [GAP id=107] to stand out. And with research showing 30 to 50 per cent of the workforce are introverts, it’s also up to employers to make space for introverts to be [GAP id=108].

questions.main_text: But Andrew Pask, a developmental biologist at the University of Melbourne who is spearheading an effort to bring back an extinct marsupial called the thylacine, or Tasmanian tiger, is unfazed by the new report and remains optimistic. “The thylacine is [SELECT id=79]”, he says. His team sequenced this predator’s genome as well as the genomes of several potential surrogate species, including the dunnart or marsupial mouse. They find that although 5% of the thylacine’s genome cannot be reconstructed, that 5% is primarily repetitive regions that [SELECT id=80].

content.text: But Andrew Pask, a developmental biologist at the University of Melbourne who is spearheading an effort to bring back an extinct marsupial called the thylacine, or Tasmanian tiger, is unfazed by the new report and remains optimistic. “The thylacine is [SELECT id=79]”, he says. His team sequenced this predator’s genome as well as the genomes of several potential surrogate species, including the dunnart or marsupial mouse. They find that although 5% of the thylacine’s genome cannot be reconstructed, that 5% is primarily repetitive regions that [SELECT id=80].

choices.text: perhaps the best candidate for de-extinction
even a more challenging species to study
interesting as it, too, lived on an island
may make the thylacine look somewhat similar to the dunnart
likely won’t affect how the transformed animal looks or behaves
could result in some repetitive behaviors in the animal

questions.main_text:
[SELECT id=82], sequencing is “steadily improving” for both modern and extinct DNA, says George Church, the Harvard University geneticist who helped found a company called Colossal Laboratories and Biosciences. “Many 100% animal genomes will be arriving faster and faster.”



Gilbert now thinks creating an exact replica of a mammoth or a passenger pigeon will be “impossible.” But such efforts might lead to ‘proxies’, animals close enough to carry out the same function in the extinct species’ old ecosystem.

content.text:
[SELECT id=82], sequencing is “steadily improving” for both modern and extinct DNA, says George Church, the Harvard University geneticist who helped found a company called Colossal Laboratories and Biosciences. “Many 100% animal genomes will be arriving faster and faster.”



Gilbert now thinks creating an exact replica of a mammoth or a passenger pigeon will be “impossible.” But such efforts might lead to ‘proxies’, animals close enough to carry out the same function in the extinct species’ old ecosystem.

questions.main_text:
#### Me, myself and I 99 p.

Ophelia, in William Shakespeare’s play *Hamlet*, says: “We know what we are, but know not what we may be.” This has been read as an expression of the uncertainty the future may bring, but it could also be seen as a suggestion for change. Can we change and how could those changes come about? Write your contribution to a contest looking for the best student essay on the topic.



*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

content.text:

Ophelia, in William Shakespeare’s play *Hamlet*, says: “We know what we are, but know not what we may be.” This has been read as an expression of the uncertainty the future may bring, but it could also be seen as a suggestion for change. questions.main_text:
#### The important things to read 99 p.

In an online discussion people are talking about reading. They argue that we are listening to audiobooks more and more, but we should actually be reading texts on paper or on screen. They also discuss what is most important to read: newspapers, fiction, non-fiction or something else. Write your contribution to the discussion and explain what you think people should be reading and why.

*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

questions.main_text:
#### The European Union: how to engage young people? 99 p.

A recent survey carried out for the European parliament found that when it comes to young people “[a] majority (55%) of respondents say they don’t understand much or anything about the EU, while 42% say they understand a great deal or a fair amount.” How should the EU approach young people to make itself more understandable? Write your answer to a follow-up survey.

*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

A recent survey carried out for the European parliament found that when it comes to young people “[a] majority (55%) of respondents say they don’t understand much or anything about the EU, while 42% say they understand a great deal or a fair amount.” How should the EU approach young people to make itself more understandable? Write your answer to a follow-up survey.

*Vastauksen pituus on enintään 1300 merkkiä. Ylityksestä seuraa pistevähennys.*

questions.main_text: [SELECT id=75], scientists would first need to sequence its genome, then edit the DNA of a close living relative to match it. Next comes the challenge of making embryos with the revised genome and bringing them to term in a living surrogate mother. So far, scientists have sequenced the genomes of about 20 extinct species, including a cave bear, passenger pigeon, and several types of mammoths and moas. But no one has yet reported re-creating the extinct genome in a living relative.

content.text: [SELECT id=75], scientists would first need to sequence its genome, then edit the DNA of a close living relative to match it. Next comes the challenge of making embryos with the revised genome and bringing them to term in a living surrogate mother. So far, scientists have sequenced the genomes of about 20 extinct species, including a cave bear, passenger pigeon, and several types of mammoths and moas. But no one has yet reported re-creating the extinct genome in a living relative.

choices.text: To decide which animal to revive
To bring back an extinct species
To discover which species are related

questions.main_text:
Marcus Stewart [SELECT id=99] the Greens of stoking a “no” campaign and urged the state parliamentary party, which has been unusually quiet on the referendum, to join the fight or risk ending up on the wrong side of history.


“I hold hope that the Greens will not only support a Voice to parliament but also campaign yes,” Stewart said. “As far as I’m [SELECT id=100], if the Greens aren’t actively campaigning yes, they are stoking a no vote among their base. History is calling. Let’s hope they show up and answer that call.”

content.text:
Marcus Stewart [SELECT id=99] the Greens of stoking a “no” campaign and urged the state parliamentary party, which has been unusually quiet on the referendum, to join the fight or risk ending up on the wrong side of history.


“I hold hope that the Greens will not only support a Voice to parliament but also campaign yes,” Stewart said. “As far as I’m [SELECT id=100], if the Greens aren’t actively campaigning yes, they are stoking a no vote among their base. History is calling. Let’s hope they show up and answer that call.”

questions.main_text:
Herridge cautions that in most cases scientists won’t know in advance how the edited genes will affect the animal’s behavior and ability to survive. Some researchers also think de-extinction efforts [SELECT id=83]. “You can save eight extant species for the cost of one that you make de-extinct,” Wendling points out.



But even those who are not sure that de-extinction could or should happen still fantasize about the possibility. Wendling wants to know what a dodo bird is like. And, Herridge says, “Personally, I would love to see a giant ground sloth or a saber-toothed cat!”

content.text:
Herridge cautions that in most cases scientists won’t know in advance how the edited genes will affect the animal’s behavior and ability to survive. Some researchers also think de-extinction efforts [SELECT id=83]. “You can save eight extant species for the cost of one that you make de-extinct,” Wendling points out.



But even those who are not sure that de-extinction could or should happen still fantasize about the possibility. Wendling wants to know what a dodo bird is like. And, Herridge says, “Personally, I would love to see a giant ground sloth or a saber-toothed cat!”

choices.text: can become increasingly more challenging technically
may take needed resources away from conservation
might result in species whose behavior is unpredictable
